title: The Ilkhanate
summary: The Ilkhanate was the Mongol state that ruled Persia, Iraq, and the Caucasus directly, and held much of Anatolia as a tributary dependency through the vassal Seljuk sultans of Rum, from 1256 to 1335 — the southwestern quarter of the fractured Mongol Empire, founded by Hulagu Khan, whose armies destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate at Baghdad in 1258 and whose later kings converted to Islam and presided over a brilliant age of Persian art and learning.
hook: In 1258 the Mongol prince Hulagu Khan took Baghdad, executed the last Abbasid caliph, and ended a line that had claimed the leadership of Islam for five centuries. Barely forty years later his great-grandson Ghazan turned toward Mecca and declared himself a Muslim, and the dynasty that had gutted the caliphate was now paying for mosques, an observatory, and the most richly painted books Persia had ever produced. The conquerors had been captured by the civilization they set out to ruin.

beginner.thePeriod:
In the winter of 1258, a Mongol army reached the walls of Baghdad. Baghdad was then the greatest city of the Muslim world. It was the home of the caliph, the man many Muslims saw as the leader of their faith. The Mongols broke into the city, killed the caliph, and left much of it in ruins. Their commander was a prince named Hulagu. He was a grandson of Genghis Khan, the founder of the Mongol Empire.

Hulagu did not ride home after the victory. He stayed and built a state of his own. It covered Persia, Iraq, and the lands around them. Today we call it the Ilkhanate. It was one of four large pieces that the Mongol Empire had broken into. Hulagu and his family would rule this piece for about eighty years.

The strange part is what happened next. The Mongols had smashed the Muslim world's proudest city. Yet within two generations their own rulers became Muslim. They paid for mosques and libraries. They hired Persian officials and artists. The conquerors slowly became part of the culture they had attacked.
beginner.theBoundaries:
Where does this state begin and end? Hulagu set out from Mongolia in the 1250s with orders from his brother, the Great Khan. His job was to spread Mongol power into the Middle East. He destroyed the mountain forts of a feared group called the Assassins. Then he took Baghdad in 1258. By 1260 he had built a realm centered on what is now Iran. Historians usually date the Ilkhanate from 1256.

The Mongols wanted more. They pushed west into Syria. But in 1260 an Egyptian army stopped them at a place called Ain Jalut. The soldiers who beat them were the Mamluks, a state run by former slave soldiers. That defeat marked the western edge of Ilkhanate power. The two sides would fight along that border for years.

The turning point came in 1335. In that year the last strong ruler, Abu Sa'id, died with no clear heir. The unified state broke apart at once. Rival families and commanders grabbed pieces of it, and new dynasties like the Jalayirids and the Chobanids ruled them. So what ended in 1335 was one Mongol authority over the whole realm. Mongol-founded rule lived on in these smaller successor states for decades.
beginner.theConditions:
How did a family of steppe warriors govern one of the oldest civilizations on earth? They could not do it alone. The Mongols were few, and Persia was full of cities, farms, and scholars. So the rulers leaned on the people they had conquered. Persian officials ran the taxes and the courts. The Mongols kept the army and the final say.

At first this worked badly. Mongol tax collectors squeezed the land hard. Farms were ruined and trade suffered. Then a ruler named Ghazan tried to fix it. Around 1300 he ordered reforms to tax more fairly and protect farmers. His chief minister was a gifted man named Rashid al-Din. Rashid al-Din was a doctor, a historian, and a statesman all at once.

The Ilkhanate also sat across the trade roads of Asia. Goods, people, and ideas moved through it. Chinese art and medicine reached Persia. Persian styles traveled east. The Mongols liked being a bridge between distant lands.
beginner.internalDiversity:
The Mongol rulers did not all believe the same things. Hulagu leaned toward Buddhism and had a Christian wife. Later rulers tried different faiths. For decades the Mongols in Persia were not Muslim at all, even though most of their subjects were.

This caused tension. A non-Muslim king ruling a Muslim land was a hard fit. The turning point came in 1295. That year Ghazan, the new ruler, became a Muslim. His subjects had waited a long time for this. His choice made the Mongols and their people share a faith at last.

The change was partly about power. Becoming Muslim helped Ghazan win support and rule more easily. Religion, for these rulers, was often a tool as much as a belief. Still, the switch was real and lasting, and from then on the Ilkhanate was officially a Muslim state. But it was not instant or total. Many Mongols held to their old ways for years, and steppe law and custom lived on beside Islam long after Ghazan.
beginner.longConsequences:
The Ilkhanate did not last long, but it left deep marks. The clearest was the loss of Baghdad. For five hundred years the city had been a center of learning and the seat of the caliph. The Mongols ended that in a single campaign. Islam would never again have one agreed leader in the old way. Power split among many rulers.

There was also a burst of creation. Ilkhanate Persia produced stunning art and books. Painters mixed Persian and Chinese styles into something new. Rashid al-Din wrote a history that tried to cover the whole known world, from China to Europe. Few books had ever aimed so wide.

And the Ilkhanate shaped what came after. Later Persian empires borrowed its ways of ruling. A century after it fell, the conqueror Tamerlane rose in the same region and claimed the Mongol name. The Ilkhanate had shown that steppe warriors could become Persian kings. That lesson outlived the state itself.
beginner.periodizationDebate:
People still argue about how to judge the Ilkhanate. Was it mainly a disaster or mainly a golden age? Both stories are true, and that is the puzzle.

The disaster is easy to see. The fall of Baghdad and years of harsh rule brought death and ruin. Some regions took generations to recover. For many people, the Mongols were simply destroyers.

But the same state also paid for a brilliant age of art, science, and history. Under Ghazan and Rashid al-Din, Persia saw new life, not just wreckage. Older accounts often picked one side. Newer ones try to hold both. The Ilkhanate destroyed a great deal and built a great deal, and it did both within the same short lifetime.

general.thePeriod:
The name itself records a hierarchy. "Il-Khan" is usually understood to mean "subordinate khan," a title that placed the Mongol rulers of Persia beneath the Great Khan who reigned in distant China. In practice the Ilkhanate governed itself, but the label captured a real fact: it was one quarter of a divided empire, not a kingdom that had grown up on its own. Hulagu Khan, a grandson of Genghis, carved it out in the 1250s on orders from the Mongol center, and his line held it until 1335. For roughly eighty years the dynasty ruled a realm reaching from Central Asia to the borders of Syria, with its heart in what is now Iran and its capital at Tabriz.

What makes the Ilkhanate unusual among conquest states is the speed of its reversal. The Mongols arrived as outsiders who had shattered the region's central institution, the Abbasid Caliphate. Within two generations their descendants had adopted the religion, the working language of government, and much of the high culture of the people they ruled. The story of the Ilkhanate is less about conquest than about what conquest becomes once the conquerors decide to stay.
general.theBoundaries:
Dating the Ilkhanate means choosing a starting point among several. Hulagu left Mongolia around 1253 with a mandate to subdue the lands to the southwest. He destroyed the mountain strongholds of the Nizari Ismailis, a sect that Western writers later called the Assassins, and in 1258 he took Baghdad and killed the last Abbasid caliph. Most accounts date the state from 1256, though a working Persian administration took shape only over the following years.

The western frontier was fixed by defeat. In 1260, after Hulagu had pulled much of his army east in response to a succession crisis, a smaller Mongol force met the Mamluks of Egypt at Ain Jalut in Galilee and lost. The battle did not end Mongol power, but it marked the limit of Ilkhanate expansion and opened a long rivalry with Cairo. For the rest of its history the state pressed against the Mamluks along the Syrian border and never broke through.

The closing date is easier to fix. Abu Sa'id, the last effective Ilkhan, died in 1335 without a clear heir. The dynasty fractured almost at once. Rival noble families and new movements carved the realm into competing successor states, and within a generation the unified Ilkhanate had ceased to exist.
general.theConditions:
The central problem of Ilkhanate rule was arithmetic. A small Mongol elite governed a dense, literate, and ancient society, and it could not do so without Persian help. Administration fell to Persian officials, while the Mongols kept the army and ultimate authority. This bargain gave the state its character, and it also gave it a recurring weakness. Early Mongol taxation was greedy and disorganized, and it damaged the farmland and trade the state depended on.

The reforms associated with Ghazan, who took power in 1295, were an attempt to repair that damage. Working with his vizier Rashid al-Din, Ghazan reorganized taxation, protected cultivators, and tried to tie the interests of the ruling class to the prosperity of the land. Rashid al-Din is the emblem of the period: a physician by training who rose to run the state and to write a history of the known world. Whether the reforms worked as well as his own account claims is debated, but the ambition was real.

The Ilkhanate also profited from its place on the map. It sat astride the routes linking the Mediterranean, Central Asia, and China, and its rulers were shareholders in that traffic. Because the Great Khan's court in China belonged to the same family, artists, physicians, and ideas moved between Persia and East Asia with unusual freedom. Chinese motifs entered Persian painting; Persian astronomy and administration traveled the other way.
general.internalDiversity:
Religion under the Ilkhanate was strikingly open before it narrowed. Hulagu favored Buddhism and had a Christian principal wife, and for four decades his successors experimented with several faiths while ruling a mostly Muslim population. This arrangement was awkward. A Buddhist or Christian king sitting over Muslim subjects lacked an obvious claim to legitimacy in their eyes, and the tension shaped court politics.

The resolution came in 1295, when Ghazan converted to Islam as he seized the throne. His conversion was bound up with a struggle for power, secured with the backing of a Mongol commander who made Islam a condition of his support. For these rulers religion often functioned as an instrument of rule rather than a private conviction, which is part of why the switch could be made so deliberately. Yet the effect was durable. After Ghazan the Ilkhanate was a Muslim state, and its later kings patronized Islamic scholarship and building.

The irony is hard to miss. Within a few decades the heirs of the men who had killed a caliph and sacked Baghdad were ruling Persia as Muslim sovereigns and funding the very religious culture their ancestors had assaulted. The conquest had run its course, and the conquered civilization had absorbed its conquerors.
general.longConsequences:
The Ilkhanate's most lasting act was also its first. The destruction of Baghdad in 1258 ended the Abbasid Caliphate as a functioning institution and removed the last widely recognized center of Sunni authority. No single figure would again command that kind of shared allegiance, and political power in the Islamic world stayed divided among competing dynasties. The loss registered as a wound in the region's memory that later writers returned to again and again.

Against that destruction stands a genuine cultural flowering. Ilkhanate patronage produced some of the finest Persian painting ever made, in which Chinese landscape conventions were absorbed into a local tradition. Rashid al-Din's world history, compiled around 1307, tried to gather the pasts of the Mongols, the Chinese, the Muslims, and the Europeans into one narrative, an early attempt at writing history on a global scale. An observatory built at Maragha drew astronomers from across the region.

The state's example outlived it. Later Persian and Central Asian rulers drew on Ilkhanate models of kingship, and the conqueror Timur, rising in the same lands two generations later, wrapped his ambitions in Mongol legitimacy. The Ilkhanate had shown that a steppe dynasty could become a Persian and Islamic one, a template that shaped the region for centuries.
general.periodizationDebate:
How to weigh the Ilkhanate remains an open question. The older picture, especially in accounts written from within the Islamic tradition, treats the Mongols as a catastrophe: the ruin of Baghdad, the killing of the caliph, and the long disruption of settled life. The scale of the early violence is not in doubt, and any honest account has to reckon with it.

A revised picture, developed by specialists on Mongol-era Iran, stresses instead a period of renewal. On this reading the Ilkhanate presided over a Persian cultural revival, drew Iran into a wider Eurasian exchange, and left art and institutions of lasting value. The historian George Lane has gone furthest, describing thirteenth-century Iran as a renaissance rather than a dark age.

Most scholars now try to hold both accounts together. The Ilkhanate was founded in destruction and matured into patronage, and the same dynasty produced both outcomes within a single lifetime. The interesting question is not which story is true, since both are, but how a state born from the sack of Baghdad came, so quickly, to sponsor the culture it had nearly destroyed.

advanced.thePeriod: The Ilkhanate occupies an unusual position in the study of the Mongol Empire because, almost uniquely among the successor states, it generated its own abundant historical literature in the language of the conquered. The Persian chronicles of Juvayni, Rashid al-Din, and Wassaf give the Ilkhanate a documentary richness that the Golden Horde, for instance, entirely lacks. Yet that richness is a trap as much as a gift. Rashid al-Din wrote as Ghazan's vizier and as the architect of the very reforms he describes, and his Compendium of Chronicles is at once the indispensable source and an interested defense of the regime that commissioned it. Reading the Ilkhanate largely through the words of its own ministers means that the state's self-image and its documented history are hard to prise apart, and much recent work has turned on learning to read these court histories against their own grain.
advanced.theBoundaries: Whether the Ilkhanate should be treated as a Mongol polity or as a Persian one is not a pedantic question, since it determines what the state is compared to and what counts as its achievement. The older tendency, framing the Ilkhans as an episode within Mongol imperial history, emphasizes continuity with Karakorum and the shared institutions of the Chinggisid world. Area specialists on Iran have pushed hard against this. George Lane's argument for a thirteenth-century Persian renaissance treats the Ilkhanate as fundamentally a chapter of Iranian history, in which a Mongol dynasty was the catalyst rather than the substance. The stakes are interpretive: read as Mongol, Ghazan's conversion looks like assimilation and a falling-away from a steppe ideal; read as Persian, the same conversion looks like the predictable reassertion of a deep-rooted civilization over its temporary masters. Neither framing is neutral, and the sources, being Persian, tilt the evidence toward the second.
advanced.theConditions: The debate over Ghazan's reforms exposes the difficulty of using a source that is also a protagonist. Rashid al-Din presents the fiscal and agrarian measures of the late 1290s as a decisive rescue of a ruined economy, and for a long time this narrative was accepted more or less at face value. More sceptical readings, developed by scholars working on the administrative and coin evidence, question both the depth of the prior collapse and the reach of the remedy, noting that the vizier had every reason to dramatize the disorder he claimed to have cured and to credit himself and his sovereign with the repair. The point generalizes: the Ilkhanate's exceptional documentation was produced by men with direct stakes in how the period would be remembered, and the quantitative claims embedded in their prose — on tax yields, on populations, on recovery — cannot be treated as neutral data. What can be said with confidence is that the reforms were attempted, and that they signalled a shift toward treating Iran as a resource to be cultivated rather than merely stripped.
advanced.internalDiversity: The conversion of the Ilkhanate to Islam has become one of the more productive problems in the field, precisely because the sources make it look easier than it was. Rashid al-Din and later Muslim writers present Ghazan's conversion in 1295 as a sincere and providential turning, a natural homecoming for a dynasty that had strayed. The confrontation that recent scholarship forces is with the political machinery behind the piety. Ghazan's conversion was extracted as the price of the emir Nawruz's military support, and it followed decades in which Ilkhanid religious policy shifted opportunistically among Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam according to diplomatic need. This does not make the conversion insincere in any simple sense, but it does mean that the tidy narrative of spiritual awakening is itself a product of the Islamic sources, which had an obvious interest in claiming the Mongols for the faith. The harder question, still unresolved, is what conversion meant on the ground, and how far the Islamization of the court preceded or lagged the Islamization of the Mongol rank and file.
advanced.longConsequences: The claim that the Ilkhanate presided over a Persian renaissance carries a polemical charge that its proponents do not always acknowledge. To call the period a renaissance is to make a judgment about net civilizational balance, weighing the manuscripts of the Great Mongol Shahnama and the astronomical tables of Maragha against the demographic and institutional destruction of the conquest. The difficulty is that these are not commensurable quantities, and the temptation to let the surviving art speak for a period whose human costs left far fewer traces is strong. There is also a problem of attribution. The Persian painting, the world history, and the observatory were produced under Mongol patronage but by Persian and other non-Mongol hands, which raises the question of whether the Ilkhanate created this culture or merely funded, and failed to prevent, it. The most defensible position treats Mongol patronage as a real and shaping condition, not incidental, while refusing to let the brilliance of the Ilkhanid court retroactively soften the violence that established it.
advanced.periodizationDebate: The deepest fault line concerns whether the Ilkhanate is best understood through the category of decline at all. The dynasty's disintegration after 1335 invites a narrative of failure — a steppe imposition that could not sustain itself once the conquering energy dissipated. But this framing imports assumptions worth examining. Charles Melville and others have shown that the collapse was less a slow decay than a sudden succession failure, contingent on Abu Sa'id's death without an heir rather than on any deep exhaustion of the state. If that is right, then the standard shape of the Ilkhanate's story — rise, apogee under Ghazan, and inevitable fall — is partly an artifact of hindsight, a pattern imposed on what was in fact a viable polity cut short by dynastic accident. The alternative reading, in which the Ilkhanate's institutions and cultural forms flowed on into the Jalayirid, Timurid, and ultimately Safavid states, dissolves the very idea of an endpoint and makes the Ilkhanate a phase in a continuous Persian political tradition rather than a discrete empire that failed.

foundation.theStory: The Ilkhanate is a case study in what happens after a conquest succeeds. A nomadic dynasty from the Mongolian steppe seized one of the oldest urban civilizations on earth, ruled it for about eighty years, and in the process was transformed by it. The historical problem is not how the Mongols won, but how a tiny foreign elite governed Persia at all, and why it ended up adopting the religion and culture of its subjects rather than imposing its own.
foundation.theDebate: Historians disagree about how to characterize the period as a whole. One tradition, rooted in the memory of the sack of Baghdad, sees the Ilkhanate primarily as destruction and disruption. Another, developed by specialists on Mongol-era Iran, sees a Persian cultural revival that produced extraordinary art, science, and history under Mongol patronage. The disagreement is partly about evidence and partly about how to weigh incommensurable things: the ruin of cities against the manuscripts and observatories the same dynasty funded.
foundation.whyItMatters: Understanding the Ilkhanate unlocks several larger stories: how the Mongol Empire fragmented into distinct regional states, how the destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate reshaped Islamic political authority, how Persian and Chinese cultures exchanged art and knowledge across Asia, and how conquering elites are often absorbed by the civilizations they conquer. It is also a key link between the medieval Persian past and the later empires, including the Safavids, that shaped modern Iran.
questions: How could a small group of Mongol conquerors rule a large, sophisticated Persian society, and why did they come to depend so heavily on the people they had defeated?

The Mongols destroyed Baghdad and killed the caliph, yet within two generations their rulers had become Muslim. What does this reversal suggest about the relationship between conquerors and the cultures they conquer?
The Ilkhanate produced both terrible destruction and a brilliant age of art and learning. How should we weigh these against each other when judging the period?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Scholarship on the Ilkhanate has shifted from treating it as an episode of Mongol imperial history toward studying it as a chapter of Iranian history. Peter Jackson's work traces the long process by which the Mongols of the Middle East moved from conquest to conversion. George Lane has argued that thirteenth-century Iran experienced a renaissance under Mongol rule rather than a collapse. Much of the debate turns on the reliability of the Persian court histories, above all Rashid al-Din's Compendium of Chronicles, which is both the richest source for the period and an official history written by the regime's own chief minister.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for the Ilkhanate is unusually rich in narrative sources and unusually poor in independent checks on them. Rashid al-Din, Juvayni, and Wassaf provide detailed accounts, but they were written by administrators close to power and shaped by their interests. Coin and document evidence can test some claims, but statements about tax revenues, populations, and economic recovery rest largely on the word of officials who had reasons to dramatize both the crisis and the cure. The evidence supports the reality of Ghazan's reforms and the flowering of Ilkhanid art; it cannot firmly establish how deep the earlier economic collapse was, or how far Islam had spread among ordinary Mongols.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong account of the Ilkhanate has to resist two easy resolutions. It cannot simply endorse the "renaissance" reading, because that lets the surviving art stand in for a period whose human costs left fewer traces and were borne by people the sources rarely name. But it also cannot reduce the Ilkhanate to the sack of Baghdad, because the same dynasty genuinely sponsored a major cultural age. The defensible position treats Mongol patronage as a real and shaping force while refusing to let it soften the violence of conquest, and it reads the court histories as evidence of what the regime wanted remembered, not as transparent fact. Ghazan's conversion is the test case: the honest reading holds the sincere and the strategic together rather than choosing one.
questions: Rashid al-Din's Compendium of Chronicles is both the best source for the Ilkhanate and a history written by its chief minister. How should a historian use a source that is also a participant in the events it describes?
If the Ilkhanate's collapse after 1335 resulted mainly from a succession failure rather than deep weakness, how much does the language of "decline" distort our understanding of the state?
The art and scholarship of the Ilkhanate were produced by Persian and other non-Mongol hands under Mongol patronage. Does the dynasty deserve credit for this culture, or only for funding it?

research.topic: The destruction of Baghdad and the end of the Abbasid Caliphate
research.content: That Hulagu's army captured Baghdad in 1258, killed the last reigning Abbasid caliph, al-Musta'sim, and ended the Abbasid Caliphate as a functioning institution is not seriously contested. The city was sacked, its population suffered heavy loss of life, and the centuries-old seat of caliphal authority was extinguished. The event is well attested across Persian, Arabic, Armenian, and later Latin sources, and it is treated by scholars as a genuine turning point in the political history of the Islamic world, after which no single recognized caliph again commanded broad Sunni allegiance in the old manner.

research.topic: The Ilkhanate as a Persian renaissance rather than a dark age
research.content: A body of scholarship on Mongol-era Iran, associated especially with George Lane, has reframed the thirteenth century as a period of cultural revival rather than unrelieved catastrophe. On this reading, Ilkhanate patronage of painting, history-writing, astronomy, and administration, together with the integration of Iran into a wider Eurasian exchange, amounts to a renaissance catalyzed by Mongol rule. The interpretation is influential but not settled: critics note that it risks letting surviving elite culture speak for a period whose destruction and human costs left fewer traces, and that much of the evidence comes from court sources with an interest in the dynasty's image.

research.topic: The true demographic and economic cost of the conquest and early Ilkhanid rule
research.content: How many people died, and how far the economy of Iran and Iraq actually collapsed under early Mongol taxation, cannot be established with confidence. What evidence would resolve it: systematic population and revenue records from before and after the conquest. Why it is unavailable: no such surveys were made, and the fullest figures come from court historians like Rashid al-Din, who had reasons to dramatize both the ruin and its repair. What can be responsibly inferred: that certain regions, notably around Baghdad and in parts of Iran, suffered severe disruption, and that recovery was uneven and slow. What must remain unknown: precise death tolls, the real depth of the economic decline as against its rhetorical exaggeration, and the regional variation with any certainty.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Wound of 1258
comparativeNarrative.content: In much of Islamic memory, the Ilkhanate begins as a wound. The Mongols sacked Baghdad in 1258. They killed the last Abbasid caliph and ended a five-century institution. Later writers returned to that disaster for centuries. From this view, the Mongols' later turn to Islam is a partial repair, not a rescue. The faith absorbed its conquerors. But the loss of the caliphate, and the splitting of Sunni authority, were never undone. This perspective sees what a focus on Ilkhanid art can miss. The dynasty's first act changed the shape of Islamic political life for good.

comparativeNarrative.name: Persia Absorbs Its Conquerors
comparativeNarrative.content: Seen from within Iran's own history, the Ilkhanate looks less like a foreign occupation. It looks more like one more time that Persia absorbed its conquerors. The Mongols arrived speaking no Persian and following other faiths. Yet within two generations they ruled in Persian, paid for Persian art, and prayed as Muslims. Ghazan's conversion in 1295, and the reforms of his vizier Rashid al-Din, mark that shift. On this reading, a deep civilization reasserted itself over its temporary masters. This vantage reveals continuity where the catastrophe story sees only rupture. The same painters, poets, and officials carried Persian culture forward, and the Ilkhanate became a bridge to the later empires of Iran.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Conquerors Who Vanished
comparativeNarrative.content: From the wider Mongol world, the Ilkhanate was never meant to be Persian. It was one quarter of a family empire. Its very name marked its place below the Great Khan in China. Its rulers were descendants of Genghis, tied to a steppe order. Judged by that standard, the path Iran's history celebrates looks like drift. The Mongols dropped their own customs. They turned to Islam. They dissolved into a settled world their founders had aimed to rule, not join. This view is a useful corrective. What one tradition reads as a homecoming, another can read as a conquering people slowly vanishing into the conquered.

comparativeSummary: These three vantages do not just disagree about whether the Ilkhanate was good or bad. They disagree about what kind of thing it was. To Islamic memory, it is a rupture defined by its first act, the destruction of Baghdad. To Iranian history, it is a story of continuity, in which Persia again absorbed its conquerors. To the Mongol world, it is a conquering dynasty dissolving into the civilization it ruled. One fact holds them together, seen from three sides. The same dynasty that ended the caliphate became a Muslim Persian house within forty years. Whether that reads as tragedy, renaissance, or disappearance depends on where the observer stood when the Mongols arrived.

rabbitHole.label: The Mongol Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Ilkhanate was the southwestern quarter of the Mongol Empire, founded by Genghis Khan's grandson Hulagu when the unified empire fragmented into four regional states.

rabbitHole.label: The Abbasid Caliphate
rabbitHole.reason: The Ilkhanate's founding act was the destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate at Baghdad in 1258, ending five centuries of caliphal authority and permanently fragmenting Sunni political leadership.

rabbitHole.label: The Mamluk Sultanate
rabbitHole.reason: The Egyptian regime of former slave soldiers that halted Ilkhanate expansion at Ain Jalut in 1260 and remained its chief rival along the Syrian frontier for decades.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: The Ilkhanate's arc runs from destroying Islam's central institution to embracing the faith: Ghazan's conversion in 1295 turned the Mongol dynasty of Persia into a Muslim state.

rabbitHole.label: Kublai Khan
rabbitHole.reason: Kublai's Yuan dynasty in China was the eastern counterpart to the Ilkhanate — another Mongol regime absorbing the civilization it ruled, and the Great Khan to whom the Ilkhans were nominally subordinate.

rabbitHole.label: Tamerlane
rabbitHole.reason: Rising in the same lands two generations after the Ilkhanate fell, Timur wrapped his conquests in Mongol legitimacy, drawing on the template of the steppe dynasty turned Persian sovereign.

rabbitHole.label: The House of Wisdom
rabbitHole.reason: Baghdad's famed center of learning was among the losses of the 1258 sack that founded the Ilkhanate, a symbol of the destruction that the dynasty's later patronage is sometimes said to have offset.

rabbitHole.label: Rashid al-Din
rabbitHole.reason: The physician, vizier, and historian who directed Ghazan's reforms and wrote the Compendium of Chronicles, the first attempt at a world history and the defining source for the Ilkhanate.

reference.author: Peter Jackson
reference.title: The Mongols and the Islamic World: From Conquest to Conversion
reference.annotation: The fullest modern treatment of exactly the arc this entry traces — how the Mongols of the Middle East moved from sacking Baghdad to ruling as Muslims. Jackson is meticulous about the politics behind Ghazan's conversion and skeptical of the sources' tidy religious narrative, which makes this the anchor for the entry's account of Islamization.

reference.author: David Morgan
reference.title: The Mongols
reference.annotation: The standard single-volume overview of the Mongol Empire, with a measured account of the Ilkhanate that neither romanticizes the conquest nor dismisses the cultural achievement. Useful here for placing the Persian khanate within the fragmented empire as a whole.

reference.author: Rashid al-Din Hamadani
reference.title: Compendium of Chronicles (Jami' al-Tawarikh)
reference.annotation: The indispensable contemporary source and, at the same time, the central problem of Ilkhanid history: written in Persian by Ghazan's own vizier, it is both the richest record of the period and an interested account by the architect of the reforms it praises. The entry leans on it while treating its quantitative claims with caution.

reference.author: George Lane
reference.title: Early Mongol Rule in Thirteenth-Century Iran: A Persian Renaissance
reference.annotation: The strongest statement of the revisionist case that Mongol Iran was a cultural revival rather than a dark age. Read for the argument the entry engages in its debate section — persuasive on Ilkhanid patronage, and worth weighing against the human costs its emphasis can understate.

reference.author: Reuven Amitai-Preiss
reference.title: Mongols and Mamluks: The Mamluk-Ilkhanid War, 1260–1281
reference.annotation: The detailed study of the Ilkhanate's long conflict with Egypt after Ain Jalut, showing how the western frontier was fixed and how the Mamluk rivalry shaped Ilkhanid diplomacy and its search for Christian allies against a shared enemy.

reference.author: Charles Melville
reference.title: The End of the Ilkhanate and After: Observations on the Collapse of the Mongol World Empire
reference.annotation: Published in The Mongols' Middle East (Brill), this chapter reframes 1335 not as the exhaustion of a failing state but as a sudden succession crisis, Abu Sa'id dying without a recognized heir, after which the realm splintered into competing successor regimes. The entry draws on it to say precisely what ended in 1335 and what carried on.

reference.author: Thomas T. Allsen
reference.title: Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia
reference.annotation: The definitive study of the cross-Eurasian exchange that ran between Ilkhanid Iran and Yuan China, tracing how astronomy, medicine, cartography, and art moved along shared family and diplomatic channels. It grounds the entry's account of the Ilkhanate as a deliberate cultural bridge rather than an accidental one.

commerce.title: The Mongols
commerce.author: David Morgan
commerce.note: Come here first if the Ilkhanate is your entry point into the Mongol world — Morgan sets the Persian khanate inside the larger empire in clear, unhurried prose that assumes nothing.

commerce.title: Daily Life in the Mongol Empire
commerce.author: George Lane
commerce.note: For the reader who wants texture over politics: how people ate, traveled, worshipped, and did business under Mongol rule, from a scholar who thinks the period was a revival rather than a ruin.

commerce.title: Making Mongol History: Rashid al-Din and the Jamiʿ al-Tawarikh
commerce.author: Stefan Kamola
commerce.note: The book to read once you realize that the entry's best source was written by a court insider — Kamola reconstructs how Rashid al-Din built his world history and why that matters for trusting it.

commerce.title: The Mongols and the Islamic World: From Conquest to Conversion
commerce.author: Peter Jackson
commerce.note: The serious reader's destination on how a dynasty that destroyed the caliphate became Muslim. Long and demanding, but unmatched on the politics behind Ghazan's conversion.

commerce.title: Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia
commerce.author: Thomas T. Allsen
commerce.note: Follow this one if the Persia-to-China exchange is what caught you — Allsen traces how artists, doctors, and ideas actually moved between the Ilkhanate and the Yuan court, and why that traffic was deliberate.

popularCulture.title: The Great Mongol Shahnama (c. 1330s)
popularCulture.description: This was a huge, richly painted copy of Persia's national epic, made at the Ilkhanate court around the 1330s. Its pictures blend Persian and Chinese styles into something new, and they show how Mongol rulers paid for Persian art. Later collectors cut the book apart and sold the pages one by one. So today we meet this masterpiece only in scattered pieces, spread across many museums.

popularCulture.title: The Dome of Soltaniyeh (completed c. 1312)
popularCulture.description: This giant blue-domed tomb was built for the Mongol ruler Öljaitü in northwest Iran. Finished around 1312, it is one of the great buildings of the Ilkhanate and still stands today. Visitors sense a powerful state behind such grandeur. The tomb cannot show how fast that state fell apart, only a generation after the dome was raised.

popularCulture.title: The Compendium of Chronicles (Jami' al-Tawarikh, c. 1307)
popularCulture.description: Rashid al-Din, the Ilkhanate's chief minister, wrote this book around 1307. It tried to tell the history of the whole known world, from China to Europe, and was filled with paintings. It is often called an early world history. But it was written for the Mongol court, so it praises the rulers and their reforms, and its picture of them must be read with care.

popularCulture.title: The Maragha Observatory (founded 1259)
popularCulture.description: Soon after taking Persia, the Mongols paid for a great observatory at Maragha, begun in 1259. Scholars there studied the stars and made new astronomy tables. The site shows that the same conquerors who wrecked cities also funded science. Little of the observatory survives now, so it is easy to imagine it as grander and calmer than the hard world around it really was.

popularCulture.title: The fall of Baghdad as a symbol of ruin
popularCulture.description: The Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258 became a lasting symbol of ruin in the Middle East. Writers and speakers still reach for it to describe later disasters and invasions. The image carries real grief and real history. Yet using it as a simple symbol can flatten a complex event, turning a long, tangled conquest into a single day of loss.
